01
How I Work

Systems Thinker
Player Coach

I specialize in technically complex systems, and the discipline of making them feel effortless.

I don't merge technology for technology's sake. I work to understand it deeply, find the real value inside it, and translate that back to every stakeholder: business, engineering, and the end user. User advocacy is non-negotiable, regardless of who's in the room. The most interesting opportunities live in the gaps: the places you only see when you've become enough of an expert to know what's missing.

Systems Thinking

I map the full system before touching a single screen. Understanding the technical and business architecture is what makes design decisions defensible.

Always Advocate for the User

No matter who's in the room, whether a VP, an engineer, or a PM, I represent the end user. That's non-negotiable in everything I do.

Player Coach

I form the idea early with product, lead ideation sessions, shape a design brief, and hand it to my team to run with. My role is to create the conditions for the team to do their best work, not to own every idea.

AI as a Tool, Not a Replacement

AI tools are powerful but no substitute for judgment, taste, and experience. I use them as a producer: pulling out the best from each and assembling something greater than the sum of its parts.

I'm a former front-end developer with a business-minded background as a startup founder and merchant. Being well-rounded isn't a nice-to-have. It's the whole point.

02
Leadership

Running a
Design Team

A safe space for open critique is the most valuable thing I can build for a team. When you come to my design review, you leave refreshed, not punished. Design critique should leave people better than it found them. The people outside the design group will challenge us hardest. So I build my team up first, so they can represent design with confidence to the wider org. It starts with them.

03
Entrepreneurial Background

Built Things
That Scaled

An entrepreneurial and DIY instinct that started as a kid building a website for his band has compounded into two acquisitions and a decade of product leadership.

I loved music just as much as design. But design has sustained me creatively on a daily basis in a way music never quite did. That DIY spirit of building something from nothing has never left.

Early Career
Fantasy Sports Betting

Took a fantasy sports betting platform from napkin idea to launch in the early days of the genre. The design and UX drew consistent public praise above competitors, a strategic win that opened the next door.

→ Acquired by larger competitor
Startup
Reverb

eBay for musical instruments. Joined at zero, helped take it to one, watched the team grow, the product flourish, and the company exit. A masterclass in building something people genuinely love.

→ Acquired by Etsy
Personal
LP Marketplace

A marketplace for vinyl records. Did the groundwork, met the community, and built a tool for people who care deeply about their collections.

→ Personal project
Active
HiFiCase

Functional objects made to be art, not art pieces made to be functional. Cases and accessories for high-fidelity audio gear, built for a community that treats their equipment like the instruments they are.

→ hificase.com (active)
)

06
Perspective

A Payments
Tourist

I've paid for things in Japan, China, Europe, and across the US. Not as a researcher. As a person trying to buy a cup of coffee.

In Japan, I stood in front of a Seven-Eleven counter and saw a wall of payment peripherals I'd never seen before. Every method represented, every device accounted for. It was the most complete expression of payment infrastructure I'd ever encountered in a convenience store. It was also completely illegible to me.

In China, I learned what it means to have money and be helpless. If you don't have WeChat Pay or Alipay set up before you arrive, linked to a Chinese bank account, you are effectively locked out of daily commerce. A cup of coffee becomes a negotiation. Not because the technology failed, but because the system wasn't designed for you. The country moved to QR codes not because its users chose them, but because the infrastructure decided for them. That's a different kind of payment story than the one we tell in the US.

In the US, we operate under a comfortable assumption: the best payment method will win. I've seen enough of the world to know that's not always true. Networks win. Governments decide. Infrastructure shapes behavior before behavior shapes infrastructure.

Payments started as a barter system. Bushels of hay for horses and milk. It evolved into this beautiful, fractured, globally inconsistent mess. I find that fascinating. And I think that's the right disposition for someone who designs for it.

07
Outside the Work

A Beginner's
Mindset

A beginner mindset is a competitive advantage. It's how expertise stays current. I've built a life around staying curious, on and off the bike.

BMX Racing

I put my BMX bike away when I got my first computer. Decades later, I'm back, racing at national championships with my son and daughter. I still use my engineering brain to learn and strategize, but the real goal is simpler: get 1% better every time I go out. Racing at nationals with my kids keeps the engineering brain sharp and the beginner's mindset honest.

Music & Audio

Music has been with me since I was making band posters in high school. Today it's vinyl, high-fidelity audio, and a good podcast while working through the details, one of the most rewarding combinations I've found. The community around serious audio gear led directly to HiFiCase. Passion has a way of turning into product.

1% better every day
builds championships.

Strategic designer
